chunks.text: The tables below contain enrollment guidelines for Computer Science classes. Please note that the enrollment guidelines on this page are subject to change. For more information about these enrollment guidelines, please view this Frequently Asked Questions page. You can also find detailed, class-specific enrollment information at classes.berkeley.edu. Be sure to read the CLASS NOTES section for the class to see semester-specific details. To see semester-specific enrollment information and FAQs, sign up for FREE to the EECS 101 section of the EDStem Discussion Dashboard. If you have questions regarding getting into CS classes, please email: cs-enrollments@eecs.berkeley.edu. To learn about declaring the CS major, visit our page, Computer Science Bachelor of Arts.
chunks.question: Where can students find detailed enrollment information for CS classes?

chunks.text: Lower Division Classes: CS 10: All undergraduate UC Berkeley students have no restrictions and can enroll or waitlist. Visiting and exchange students can enroll or waitlist with no restrictions. Graduate UC Berkeley students cannot enroll or waitlist in undergraduate classes. All MEng students cannot enroll or waitlist in undergraduate classes during the academic year. Concurrent enrollment applications are processed starting week 2 of classes.
chunks.question: Who cannot enroll in CS 10 during the academic year?

chunks.text: CS 61A: Most seats are reserved for specific student populations. Unreserved seats are available to any UC Berkeley undergraduate student. Visiting and exchange students may be waitlisted. Graduate UC Berkeley students can waitlist and are eligible to enroll in remaining seats. All MEng students cannot enroll or waitlist in undergraduate classes. Concurrent enrollment applications are processed starting week 2 of classes.
chunks.question: What happens to waitlisted students in CS 61A?
chunks.answer: They can enroll in remaining seats automatically.

chunks.text: CS 61B: Most seats are reserved for specific student populations. Unreserved seats are available to any UC Berkeley undergraduate or visiting and exchange student. Graduate UC Berkeley students can waitlist and are eligible to enroll in remaining seats. All MEng students cannot enroll or waitlist in undergraduate classes. Concurrent enrollment applications are processed starting week 2 of classes.
chunks.question: Who can access unreserved seats in CS 61B?

chunks.text: CS 61C: Declared EECS, ECE, and CompSci majors have no restrictions and can enroll or waitlist. Non-EECS COE majors, L&S undeclared, and other declared majors cannot enroll or waitlist during the academic year. Graduate UC Berkeley students and all MEng students cannot enroll or waitlist in undergraduate classes. Concurrent enrollment applications are processed starting week 2 of classes.
chunks.question: Which majors have no enrollment restrictions for CS 61C?

chunks.text: Bridge Classes in EECS: Courses such as EECS 47D, COMPSCI 47B, or COMPSCI 47C are referred to as bridge courses. They are not actual courses but an option for students who have completed some material in a regular course. The instructor determines how much of the course to complete via the bridge course. Bridge courses must be completed for a letter grade and provide units (usually 1-2).
chunks.question: What are bridge courses in EECS?
chunks.answer: Options to complete portions of regular courses.

chunks.text: For bridge courses: All COE students interested in CS 47B should contact Olivia Chan in ESS. For other EECS bridge courses, contact your ESS Adviser. All non-COE students interested in CompSci bridge courses should contact cs-advising@cs.berkeley.edu. For EECS bridge courses, contact eecs-advising@eecs.berkeley.edu.
chunks.question: Who should non-COE students contact for CompSci bridge courses?

chunks.text: CS 70: EECS/EECS joint majors, ECE majors, or ENG Undeclared have no restrictions and can enroll or waitlist. CompSci majors, non-EECS COE majors, L&S undeclared (terms 3-4) can waitlist only. All other full-time UC Berkeley students can waitlist and enroll based on waitlist position. Graduate UC Berkeley students can waitlist and are eligible for remaining seats. All MEng students cannot enroll or waitlist during the academic year. Concurrent enrollment applications are processed starting week 2 of classes.
chunks.question: What enrollment option do CompSci majors have for CS 70?

chunks.text: Upper Division Classes: Open Upper Division Classes include CS 168 and CS 188, open to any UC Berkeley or visiting undergraduate students. All seats are reserved for specific populations until the first day of class. Waitlisted students can enroll in remaining seats automatically. Graduate and MEng students cannot enroll or waitlist in undergraduate classes. Concurrent enrollment applications are processed starting week 2 of classes.
chunks.question: Which upper division CS classes are open to all undergraduates?

chunks.text: Restricted Upper Division Classes: Only CS 168 and CS 188 are open; all others are restricted. Declared EECS, ECE, and CompSci majors have no restrictions. Data Science majors have restricted enrollment in specific classes. L&S undeclared students cannot enroll or waitlist during the academic year. Other declared majors, EECS graduate students, non-EECS graduate students, and all MEng students have restrictions. Visiting and exchange students cannot enroll or waitlist during the academic year. Concurrent enrollment applications are processed starting week 2 of classes.
chunks.question: Who cannot enroll in restricted upper division CS classes?

chunks.text: Graduate Level Classes: EECS graduate students have no restrictions. EECS MEng students have restricted enrollment to a few classes. Non-EECS graduate students can waitlist but enrollment is not guaranteed. Non-EECS MEng students are prevented from waitlisting or enrolling. Any UC Berkeley undergraduate is blocked from enrolling or waitlisting. Concurrent enrollment applications are processed starting week 2 of classes. Visiting and exchange students cannot enroll or waitlist during the academic year.
chunks.question: What is the enrollment policy for non-EECS graduate students?

chunks.text: Time Conflict Policy: Some classes may permit time conflicts with lectures, labs, or discussion sections. However, these classes will not offer alternate exams unless confirmed by the instructor. Class notes indicate whether a class permits time-conflict enrollment. It is the student's responsibility to ensure they can take both final exams. If students cannot avoid a final exam conflict, they should not enroll in both classes.
chunks.question: What should students do if they have a final exam conflict?

chunks.text: Prerequisite Policy: Prerequisites for CS classes are generally strongly recommended and not formally enforced. Specific policies are in the Class Notes on classes.berkeley.edu. Formally enforced prerequisites require completion with a passing grade. Informally enforced prerequisites allow enrollment but faculty may drop students. Co-requisites: students taking prerequisites concurrently may be dropped at the instructor's request.
chunks.question: Where can students find prerequisite policies for CS classes?

chunks.text: For questions, contact cs-enrollments@eecs.berkeley.edu. Resources include information for current students, faculty and staff, center for student affairs, visiting research scholars, current graduate students, current undergraduate students, ASEs, facilities, safety, space management, and login URLs.
chunks.question: Who should be contacted for enrollment questions?
